coredata.dc:title: A versatile platform for colorimetric, fluorescence and photothermal multi-mode glyphosate sensing by carbon dots anchoring ferrocene metal-organic framework nanosheet

coredata.dc:description: Concerns regarding pesticide residues have driven attempts to exploit accurate, prompt and straightforward approaches for food safety pre-warning. Herein, a nanozyme-mediated versatile platform with multiplex signal response (colorimetric, fluorescence and temperature) was proposed for visual, sensitive and portable detection of glyphosate (GLP). The platform was constructed based on a N-CDs/FMOF-Zr nanosensor that prepared by in situ anchoring nitrogen-doped carbon dots onto zirconium-based ferrocene metal-organic framework nanosheets. The N-CDs/FMOF-Zr possessed excellent peroxidase (POD)-like activity and thus could oxide colorless 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into a blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) in presence of H2O2. Intriguingly, owing to the blocking effect triggered by multiple interaction between GLP and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, its POD-like activity of the latter was remarkably suppressed, which can modulate the transformation of TMB into oxTMB, generating tri-signal responses of fluorescence enhancement, absorbance and temperature decrease. More significantly, the temperature mode can be facilely realized by a portable home-made mini-photothermal device and handheld thermometers. The proposed multimodal sensing was capable of providing sensitive results by fluorescence mode and simultaneously realized visual/portable testing by colorimetric and photothermal channels. Consequently, it exhibited more adaptability for practical applications, which can satisfy different testing requirements according to sensitivity and available instruments/meters, presenting a new horizon for exploiting multifunctional sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology characterizations of FMOF-Zr and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr: SEM (A), TEM (B) and HR-TEM (C) images of FMOF-Zr; SEM (D), TEM (E) and HR-TEM (F) images of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr; (G) EDS-element mappings of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr and corresponding elements of C, N, O, Fe and Zr.
XRD patterns (A), FTIR spectra (B), XPS survey spectra (C) of different materials; The deconvoluted spectra of Zr3d (D) and Fe2p (E) in FMOF-Zr and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr.

(A) UV–vis spectra of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr in NaAc-HAc buffer, TMB, H2O2 and TMB+H2O2 +NaAc-Hc system; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr in 2 mmo/L ABTS and OPD solution in the presence of H2O2 (solid line) and the spectrum of ABTS and OPD solution only (dashed line); The effect of pH (C) and temperature (D) on POD-like activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. Inset: corresponding colorimetric photos from left to right.

The steady-state kinetic curves of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr toward TMB (A) and H2O2 (B); The corresponding double-reciprocal plots of TMB (C) and H2O2 (D); (E) The relative activity of different scavengers on N-CDs/FMOF-Zr +TMB+H2O2 (n = 3); (F) EPR spectra of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr +H2O2 system sample.

The UV–vis absorbance spectra (A) and corresponding linear fitting curve (B) of colorimetric mode of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr+TMB+H2O2 sensing system in the presence of different concentrations of GLP; The inset of A: side colorimetric photographs; (C) the A652 of nine sample solutions at the same concentration of 1 µg/mL GLP. The fluorescent spectra (D) and linear fitting curve (E) of fluorescent mode; (F) the F406 for duplicate detection of 1 µg/mL GLP (n = 9).

(A) Illustration of the proposed photothermal device for detecting GLP based on thermometers readout; (B) Feasibility verification of the photothermal GLP sensing platform: ΔT of different system including (1) NaAc-HAc buffer, N-CDs/FMOF-Zr in TMB+H2O2 +NaAc-HAc system before (2) and after interacting with GLP (3); (C) ΔT of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr+TMB+H2O2 +NaAc-HAc sensing system versus different irradiation time; (D) Relationship between ΔT and GLP concentrations (0–6.38 µg/mL); (E) A linear curve between ΔT and GLP concentration in 0.016–3.98 µg/mL; (F) ΔT of nine sample solutions at the same concentration of 1 µg/mL GLP.
XRD patterns (A), FTIR spectra (B), XPS survey spectra (C) and high-resolution spectra of N1s (D), O1s (E) and Zr3d (F) in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr before and after interacting with GLP.
Schematic preparation of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr nanozyme and construction a colorimetric, fluorescence and photothermal temperature “three-in-one” sensing platform.

head.ce:title: A versatile platform for colorimetric, fluorescence and photothermal multi-mode glyphosate sensing by carbon dots anchoring ferrocene metal-organic framework nanosheet

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Concerns regarding pesticide residues have driven attempts to exploit accurate, prompt and straightforward approaches for food safety pre-warning. Herein, a nanozyme-mediated versatile platform with multiplex signal response (colorimetric, fluorescence and temperature) was proposed for visual, sensitive and portable detection of glyphosate (GLP). The platform was constructed based on a N-CDs/FMOF-Zr nanosensor that prepared by in situ anchoring nitrogen-doped carbon dots onto zirconium-based ferrocene metal-organic framework nanosheets. The N-CDs/FMOF-Zr possessed excellent peroxidase (POD)-like activity and thus could oxide colorless 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into a blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) in presence of H2O2. Intriguingly, owing to the blocking effect triggered by multiple interaction between GLP and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, its POD-like activity of the latter was remarkably suppressed, which can modulate the transformation of TMB into oxTMB, generating tri-signal responses of fluorescence enhancement, absorbance and temperature decrease. More significantly, the temperature mode can be facilely realized by a portable home-made mini-photothermal device and handheld thermometers. The proposed multimodal sensing was capable of providing sensitive results by fluorescence mode and simultaneously realized visual/portable testing by colorimetric and photothermal channels. Consequently, it exhibited more adaptability for practical applications, which can satisfy different testing requirements according to sensitivity and available instruments/meters, presenting a new horizon for exploiting multifunctional sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate (GLP) is an extensively applied organophosphorus herbicide worldwide because it can act on 5-enolpyruvylshikimate-3-phosphate synthase to suppress the synthesis of three aromatic amino acids (phenylalanine, tyrosine and tryptophan) (Leino et al., 2021). Whereas, excessive GLP residue may cause many health-related incidents such as depression, infertility, Alzheimer and Parkinson’s disease, and even cancer (Sarkar and Das, 2017; Valle et al., 2019). So far, several analytical approaches, including gas chromatography (Tseng et al., 2004), high-performance liquid chromatography (Wang S. et al., 2016), ion chromatography (Zhu et al., 1999), chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (Royer et al., 2000) and capillary electrophoresis (Rojano-Delgado et al., 2010) with low LOD of 0.02 μg/mL, 0.24 μg/L, 0.042 μg/mL, 0.025 μg/L and 0.1 μg/mL, have emerged as effective methods to detect GLP. These techniques possessed high sensitivity and accuracy, but they usually require expensive instruments, complicated pre-treatments and skilled technicians. Furthermore, it is inaccessible to implant these approaches into a portable device for in-field testing. These shortcomings seriously impede their applications in rapid on-site screening, which encouraged efforts to explore low-cost, easy-handling, rapid readout and portable sensing platform for GLP.

With regard to various assay methods, colorimetric sensing analysis is in fore because of its advantages in low cost, easy manipulation and visual readout (He et al., 2022; Li N. et al., 2021). However, single colorimetric typically have limited sensitivity (Liang et al., 2022) and may be vulnerable to interferences from environmental factors, resulting in inaccurate readouts (Su et al., 2021). Combining colorimetric signal with other sensing modalities to construct a multiple-signal integrated platform may be an alluring protocol to immensely improve sensing performance (Liang et al., 2022). For comparison, a multi-mode approach commendably inherits visualization of the colorimetric mode, and simultaneously introduces diverse quantitative modalities with different sensitivity to augment the applicability of overall sensing (Yang et al., 2022). Accordingly, multi-mode can satisfy different testing requirements according to sensitivity and available instruments/meters (Li et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2022). Additionally, the multi-modal exhibits a built-in cross reference correction via mutual confirmation between different modes to considerably improve reliability (Li et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2022). In this regard, fluorescence (FL) and colorimetric dual-mode sensing have been generally established owing to the high sensitivity of FL analysis and visual results of colorimetric (Guan et al., 2021; Wei D. et al., 2021), but background color and autofluorescence still hampered their performance (Ding et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2021). Notably, photothermal temperature signal, featured with the conversion of light to heat (temperature) by photothermal materials/agents, have been fancifully emerged as a powerful supplementary for colorimetry and FL readouts to develop novel assays. The photothermal output can be straightforwardly read by a portable and affordable thermometer, which is user-friendly and easy-to-handle, readily realizing the miniaturization of the device (Ding et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2021). Thus, the photothermal sensor cannot be constrained by time, place or professional operators (Wei M. et al., 2021). More significantly, photothermal sensors possess lower background due to that the temperature change mainly induced by photothermal effect (Guo et al., 2020) and therefore could address the color resolution restrictions of colorimetric and autofluorescence (Luo et al., 2020). Inspired by these, developing a colorimetric, FL and photothermal readouts integrated platform is expected to simultaneously realize portable, visual and ultrasensitive multi-mode assay.

On the other hand, improving sensitivity is of utmost importance for trace detection of contaminants considering their low residue in samples. Nanozymes, nanomaterials with biomimetic enzyme activities, have grown in popularity for sensing due to their enzyme-catalyzed signal amplification (Liang et al., 2022). Besides, the pre-concentration effect achieved by incorporating nanomaterials has also been proven to be a potent tool for improving sensitivity (Zhou et al., 2018). Hence, developing a bifunctional sensor with enzyme catalytic activity and a strong pre-concentration effect could be anticipated to realize sensitive detection. Metal-organic framework (MOF), a kind of porous crystalline material constructed from the self-assembly of metal nodes and organic linkers, has been identified as a promising candidate for the development of nanozymes (Huang et al., 2021). Thereinto, 2D MOF nanosheets outperformed bulk or other morphology MOFs in pre-concentration and catalytic performance owing to their larger surface area and more accessible active sites (Deng et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). Besides, developing its heterogeneous composites of MOF by incorporating other nanomaterials (e. g. carbon dots, metal oxides) was an effective strategy to further improve pristine MOF enzyme activity (Tang et al., 2018).

Motivated by the afore-mentioned considerations, we constructed a nanozyme-mediated multi-mode platform ( Scheme 1) for multiple signals (FL, temperature and colorimetric) detection of GLP. Besides, for further miniaturization of photothermal detection, a home-made photothermal device consisting of three separate slots for 96-well plates, a 3D printed sample bracket, 808 nm lasers, and thermometers, was also constructed for high throughput assay. The nanozyme (N-CDs/FMOF-Zr) was synthesized by hybridizing a N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with zirconium-based ferrocene MOF nanosheets (N-CDs/FMOF-Zr). The N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, which possessed two metal nodes (Zr and Fe), displayed an intriguing peroxidase (POD)-like activity to oxide colorless TMB into a blue oxTMB with the participation of H2O2. The Zr6O8 clusters not only coordinate with ferrocene to form stable MOF nanosheets for pre-concentration of GLP but also serve as specific binding sites for selective recognition of GLP. Owing to powerful pre-concentration of MOF nanosheets, GLP can be promptly anchored onto the surface of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, consequently suppressing its POD activity and further compromising the catalytic oxidation of TMB to oxTMB. Accordingly, the increased GLP can lead to different signal changes, including FL enhancement, temperature and absorbance decrease. This versatile platform not only enables instrument-free testing in fields but also provides accurate analysis in laboratories, providing a novel manner for realizing multifunctional sensing.
Detailed information regarding materials and characterizations was shown in Supporting Information (SI).

The N-CDs was synthesized by hydrothermally carbonizing citric acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (Tris) according to a previous literature with certain modifications (Liu et al., 2017). Detailed information was given in SI.

N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was synthesized by in situ loading N-CDs onto FMOF-Zr. Specifically, 0.137 g FcDA, 0.117 g ZrCl4 and 1.5 mL HAc were mixed with 15 mL DMF under stirring until dissolved at room temperature. And then, N-CDs with different volumes were dropwise added into the above mixture under vigorous magnetic stirring, followed by continued stirring for 2 h at room temperature. Thereafter, the resultant reaction system was placed in a 50 mL autoclave for thermal reaction (120 °C, 12 h). After the reaction, the black N-CDs/FMOF-Zr were obtained and successively washed three times with DMF and H2O by centrifuging (8000 rpm, 30 min). The corresponding products were named as N-CDs/FMOF-Zr(X) (X = 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 4), where the X represents the volume of N-CDs (mL). FMOF-Zr was also prepared in identical conditions as N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, but without the addition of N-CDs (Fang et al., 2021).

To inspect the POD-like catalytic activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr nanosheets, 15 µL N-CDs/FMOF-Zr (0.65 mg/mL) was mixed with 1 mL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH=4.0, 0.1 mol/L). Then, 50 µL TMB (2 mmol/L) and 50 µL H2O2 (50 mmol/L) were added into the catalytic system. After incubating for 10 min at room temperature, the absorbance at a wavelength of 652 nm (A652) was monitored as an indicator of POD activity.

The related kinetic parameters for POD activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr were investigated by recording A652 at varied concentrations of TMB under a fixed content of H2O2. Similarly, the corresponding kinetics of H2O2 was also measured by fixing the concentration of TMB. The K
m and V
max can be determined via the Michaelis–Menten equation (Sun et al., 2022), which was expressed as the following Eq. 1.

As we all know, similar to bio-enzyme, enzyme-like activity of a certain nanozyme was closely related to nanozyme dosage, substrate concentrations and other environment factors (e. g. pH and ion strength) (Luo et al., 2022), which could ultimately influence sensing performance. On the other hand, the incubation time for nanozyme and GLP interaction also may affect detection because insufficient reaction time may lead to low sensitivity and redundant reaction time was not profitable to rapid sample-to-answer. Accordingly, the detection parameters, including pH and concentration of buffer, N-CDs/FMOF-Zr concentration, substrate (TMB and H2O2) concentrations and incubation time, were systematically optimized to achieve the optimal detection performance. Considering that all signal readouts were revenant to the transformation between TMB and oxTMB, thus the UV–vis absorbance change of oxTMB at 652 nm (ΔA=A0-A, A0 and A were the absorbance in the absence and presence of GLP) was chosen as an indicator to optimize all these parameters according to single factor experiments.

Typically, 15 µL 0.65 mg/mL N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was mixed with 100 µL varied concentrations of GLP in 1 mL HAc-NaAc buffer (10 mmol/L, pH=4.0) and incubated at room temperature for 5 min. Afterward, 2 mmol/L TMB and 50 mmol/L H2O2 were added into these systems to undergo a chromogenic reaction at 35 °C for 10 min. Finally, the solution color, FL spectra, UV–vis absorbance spectra and temperature were recorded.

In terms of the colorimetric sensing channel, we used a microplate reader and a smartphone (Huawei nova5 iPro, Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China) to obtain UV–vis absorbance spectra and colorimetric photos. Similarly, FL spectra were collected under an excitation and emission slit width of 10 nm to investigate FL sensing performance.

Subsequently, the temperature readout mode was investigated under the irradiation of 808 nm lasers with a power density of 2 W/cm2. Meanwhile, the corresponding temperature changes (ΔT) of different sensing systems with varied GLP concentrations were monitored by pen-style thermometers. It should be noted that the ΔT referred to an absolute temperature increment of a certain sensing system temperature before and after irradiation.

To evaluate the selectivity of the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr nanozyme-mediated sensing platform, different interferents including ions, amino acids, saccharides and other pesticides with respective 100-fold, 10-fold, 5-fold and equivalent concentrations of GLP were mixed with N-CDs/FMOF-Zr and followed by incubating for 5 min in HAc-NaAc buffer at room temperature. Subsequently, these systems containing interferences were incubated with 2 mmol/L TMB and 50 mmol/L H2O2 at 35 °C for 10 min. Thereafter, sensing solution color and UV–vis absorbance spectra were accordingly recorded. The anti-interference ability was also investigated with a similar process to the selectivity test, except that interferents were added to GLP-containing systems. The reusability test of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was investigated by successively using ethanol and 10 mmol/L NaOH as eluents for several times to remove the absorbed oxTMB and GLP. After that, N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was washed with H2O several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of the multi-mode platform
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the feasibility of the versatile multi-mode sensing platform in practical application, a standard addition method was used to quantify the content of GLP in real food samples (rice, millet and soybean). These samples were pretreated by a modified QuEChERS method (Jimenez-Lopez et al., 2020), and the detailed information was presented in SI. After pretreatment, each sample was spiked with GLP standard solution to obtain final concentrations of 0.5, 1, and 2 µg/mL, and then multimode detection was performed with the above method under the same parameters.

Finally, the obtained results were compared with those of conventional HPLC-MS/MS. Regarding the HPLC-MS/MS analysis, sample preparation was performed as follows: 2 g samples were mixed with 200 µL 1.0 µg/mL 1, 2-C13N15 isotope-labeled internal standard, 10 mL H2O and 15 mL methylene chloride. The mixture was vortexed for 20 min and then was centrifuged to collect supernatant, which was then mixed with acidity regulator (a mixture of 16 g KH2PO4 in 160 mL H2O, 13.4 mL HCl and 40 mL methanol). After the CAX column was activated with 10 mL H2O, the extract was added and washed twice with 0.7 mL of CAX eluent, then eluted with 11 mL of CAX eluent (a mixture of 160 mL H2O, 2.7 mL HCl and 40 mL methanol) and collected. The eluate was evaporated to dryness under reduced pressure at 45 °C, and 1 mL of 5% borate buffer was added to dissolve the residue, which was adjusted to pH= 9 with 20% potassium hydroxide solution, 3 mol/L HCl and 0.3 mol/L HCl. Finally, the purified extracting solution were mixed with 200 µL of 1.0 g/L 9-Fluorenylmethyl chloroformate acetone solution and then derivatized at room temperature overnight. The obtained solutions were filtered using a 0.22 µm microporous membrane for HPLC-MS/MS analysis. The HPLC-MS/MS analysis was performed on an AB 4500 Liquid chromatography-tandem quadrupole mass spectrometer under the following chromatographic conditions: Waters C18 chromatographic column, flow rate of 0.4 mL/min, 10 µL of injection volume and ion source of electrospray ionization (ESI+) at a temperature of 550 °C. A mixture of acetonitrile and 0.1% formic acid water was used as a mobile phase.

All FL, photothermal temperature and colorimetric tests were performed in triplicate for statistical analysis using SPSS 23.0 software. The statistical difference between groups was evaluated by a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Tukey-HSD. Besides, P < 0.05 was regarded as statistically significant.

The N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was synthesized by in situ hybridizing N-CDs onto FMOF-Zr via a one-pot solvothermal approach. Interestingly, Fig. S1 revealed that the introduction of N-CDs can greatly increase the catalytic oxidation activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr toward TMB. And the corresponding activity increased with the increase of N-CDs content, the enhanced catalytic mechanism would be discussed in detail in Section 3.2. For obtaining optimal sensing performance, the content of N-CDs for synthesis of FMOF-Zr was optimized. As depicted in Fig. S2, the maximum change of A652 before and after incubation with GLP was obtained when 0.5 mL N-CDs was used to prepare N-CDs/FMOF-Zr (P < 0.05).

The SEM and TEM images implied that the as-prepared FMOF-Zr ( Fig. 1 A and Fig. 1 B) were rectangle-shaped nanosheets with irregular layered morphology, and its lateral size ranged from 460 to 810 nm. Moreover, the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1 C) revealed that FMOF-Zr nanosheets possessed legible lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 1.42 nm, which corresponded to the (100) planes of the FMOF-Zr crystal (Deng et al., 2019). After the introduction of N-CDs, the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr composite still displayed nanosheet morphology (Fig. 1 D and 1 E) with obviously decreased size and N-CDs were anchored onto FMOF-Zr (Fig. 1 F). Aside from the lattice fringes of FMOF-Zr (1.42 nm), the HR-TEM image of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr (inset of Fig. 1 F) also displayed obvious lattice stripes of N-CDs (0.22 nm), which can be ascribed to the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon (Liu et al., 2017). The co-existence of these two kinds of lattice fringes implied the presence of N-CDs in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, which was similar to previous literatures (Gu et al., 2019; Su et al., 2020). Further, EDX energy spectrum (Fig. S3) and EDS-element mappings (Fig. S4) demonstrated that C, O, Fe and Zr elements were distinctly presented in FMOF-Zr. Besides these elements, an extra N element also appeared in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr (Fig. S5) due to the introduction of N-containing N-CDs. This can be further verified by EDS-element mappings of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr (Fig. 1 G). The TGA curve of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was depicted in Fig. S6 , in which the initial weight loss from 30 °C to 100 °C can be ascribed to the removal of H2O due to the dehydration of Zr6 clusters. Further, the destruction of coordinating bonds between FcDA ligand and Zr6 clusters resulted in a conspicuous weight decrease at 200–350 °C. The significant weight loss over 450 °C was generated by its framework collapse. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was investigated to reveal its surface area and porosity properties. Fig. S7A depicted an archetypical reversible type-I N2 isotherm of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, demonstrating its micropore property. Meanwhile, the H4 type hysteresis loops also indicated the presence of mesopores (Fang et al., 2021). These results indicated that micropore and mesopores coexisted in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, which can be further proved by the pore size distribution (Fig. S7B). In addition, surface area and total pore volume of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr were respectively calculated to be ∼ 191.9855 m2 g−1 and 0.1013 cm3 g−1. The large surface area and nanoporous properties of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr were conducive to improve the pre-concentration effect of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr toward GLP.

The XRD patterns of different materials were shown in Fig. 2 A. The pattern of FMOF-Zr nanosheets was well matched with the simulated one from its single crystal (Fang et al., 2021), indicating the successful formation of FMOF-Zr. In terms of N-CDs, its XRD pattern showed a broad peak at 2θ= 21.55° (Fig. S8), corresponding to the (002) plane of a graphitic structure (Liu et al., 2017). After integration of N-CDs, the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr commendably inherited the crystalline structure of FMOF-Zr, indicating that N-CDs anchored onto FMOF-Zr without changing its crystal nanostructure. The absence of the N-CDs pattern may be due to its ultra-small size along with low content and uniform distribution of N-CDs in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, which was similar to previous studies (Li et al., 2020; Xia et al., 2021). In addition, the peak intensity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was lower than that of pristine FMOF-Zr, which can be explained by the decreased periodicity of MOFs crystal lattices after the introduction of N-CDs (Li et al., 2020). FT-IR spectra of FMOF-Zr and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr were depicted in Fig. 2 B. A wide adsorption of FMOF-Zr in the range of 3100–3600 cm−1 was on account of the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups. Characteristic peaks in 1635–1571 cm−1 and 1394–1332 cm−1 were attributed to CO asymmetric (δ C
O ) and symmetric vibrations of ferrocenedicarboxylic groups (Liang et al., 2021; Qiang et al., 2022). The peaks at 1112 and 970 cm−1 were assigned to cyclopentadiene in the ferrocene ligand (Gao et al., 2020). The in-plane bending and stretching vibrations of Zr-O were evidenced by the peaks at 775 and 680 cm−1. And the peak at 565 cm−1 was associated with the Fe-O bond. These peaks can be still observed in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, but the δ C
O changed after anchoring N-CDs, which indicated that carboxylate groups were involved in the hybrid process. The XPS patterns (Fig. 2 C) demonstrated that FMOF-Zr was comprised of Zr3d (186.08 eV), C1s (284.08 eV), O1s (531.08 eV) and Fe2P (707.08 eV), and an additional N1s peak at 401.08 eV for N-CDs/FMOF-Zr due to the introduction of N-containing N-CDs. This was in accordance with the afore-mentioned results of EDS and element mapping. The deconvoluted Zr3d spectra (Fig. 2 D) of FMOF-Zr possessed two peaks at 182.10 and 184.45 eV, which were derived from Zr3d5/2 and Zr3d5/2 of Zr-OH. The high-resolution spectrum of Fe2P (Fig. 2 E) was fitted with 2p1/2 peaks (Fe2+: 707.13 eV; Fe3+: 707.64 eV) and 2p3/2 peaks (Fe2+: 720.15 eV; Fe3+: 720.77 eV) (Fang et al., 2021). After hybridizing with N-CDs, the proportion of each element changed compared with that of FMOF-Zr (Table S1). Additionally, both the Zr3d (Fig. 2 D) and Fe2p (Fig. 2 E) deconvoluted spectra displayed blue-shift trends for N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. This may be caused by electron-donation effect of oxygen containing groups of N-CDs with electron-donating ability, causing an increase in atoms electron density. To sum up, these results of SEM, TEM, EDS, XRD, FTIR and XPS mutually implied that the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was triumphantly synthesized via a straightforward one-pot approach.

Herein, TMB was chosen as a model chromatic agent to investigate catalytic performance of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. It can be observed from Fig. 3 A that N-CDs/FMOF-Zr displayed an obvious absorbance at 652 nm (A652) in TMB+H2O2 +NaAc-HAc buffer system, but no obvious absorbance in sole NaAc-HAc buffer, TMB and H2O2. Accordingly, the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr+TMB+H2O2 system presented a bright blue color due to the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB while other systems were transparent (inset of Fig. 3 A), indicating intrinsic peroxidase (POD)-like activity. Furthermore, catalytic oxidation of other two chromogenic substrates of 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 3 B) further evidenced its POD-like activity. It is well known that enzyme activity is closely related to environmental pH and temperature. From Fig. 3 C, the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr possessed the highest POD-like activity at pH= 4.0. Besides, temperatures also had a significant effect on its catalytic activity and obtained the maximum at 35 ◦C (P < 0.05) (Fig. 3 D), which was very close to the optimal temperature of nature enzyme for catalytic. Furthermore, POD activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr displayed an unsignificant decrease (P > 0.05) during 30 days at room temperature (Fig. S9), demonstrating its superior stability. These results indicated that the as-prepared N-CDs/FMOF-Zr possessed excellent POD catalytic activity, which can be developed as a robust nanozyme-based sensing platform.

Subsequently, steady-state kinetic studies were conducted by monitoring the POD-like catalytic performance of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr at varied concentrations of TMB and H2O2, which including maximum reaction velocity (V
max ) and Michaelis constant (K
m ). It can be observed from Fig. 4 A and 4B that both the catalytic curves of TMB and H2O2 by N-CDs/FMOF-Zr were hyperbolic, which was consistent with the feature of Michaelis-Menten reactions. And then double reciprocal plots (Fig. 4 C and 4D) were obtained by fitting 1/V versus 1/TMB or 1/H2O2 concentration. The K
m values of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr toward TMB and H2O2 were 0.37 and 1.45 mM, which were lower than those of other nanozymes (Table S2 and S3), implying better affinity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr towards TMB and H2O2. Meanwhile, the corresponding V
max for TMB and H2O2 were 1.32 and 0.33 μM/s, which were higher than those of most other nanozymes, implying that N-CDs/FMOF-Zr possessed a rapid reaction rate and a higher catalytic rate.

The catalytic oxidation mechanism of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr toward TMB in the presence of H2O2 was further investigated. Several previous researches have proved that redox electron cycling between different valence states of metal centers is critical to MOF-based nanozymes' catalytic activity, and this process would generate different types of reactive oxygen radicals (ROS) (Luo et al., 2019). Radical scavenging experiments were conducted using three different radical scavengers, including thiourea for hydroxyl radical (•OH), 1,4-Benzoquinone (PBQ) for superoxide anion (•O2 −) and histidine for singlet oxygen (1O2). As shown in Fig. 4 E, the POD-like activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr obviously decreased after the addition of thiourea with different concentrations, indicating the dominating participation of •OH during this catalytic process. Meanwhile, the activity also displayed a downward trend when using PBQ and histidine as scavengers. Further, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy (Fig. 4 F) demonstrated the characteristic pairs of •OH, •O2 − and 1O2, revealing the generation of •OH, •O2 − and 1O2 during the catalytic process. Therefore, multiple ROS were involved in the catalytic activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. XPS was conducted to reveal electron recycling resulted from different valence states of metal centers. In comparison, the deconvoluted Fe2p spectrum of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr (refer to Fig. 2 E) showed that the Fe3+ and Fe2+ species still co-existed, providing strong evidence for electron recycling between Fe3+ and Fe2+. As a result, the electron recycling between a redox pair of Fe2+/Fe3+ enabled N-CDs/FMOF-Zr to be served as a Fenton catalyst to display POD-like activity (Tai et al., 2022).

The enhanced catalytic mechanism of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr compared with FMOF-Zr was also studied. Firstly, N-CDs also possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity (Fig. S10), endowing N-CDs/FMOF-Zr with superimposed catalytic activity. Similar results regarding the POD activity of N-CDs were also revealed by other literature (Kitchawengkul et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2021). Besides, the catalytic activities of nanomaterials are usually associated with their morphology, pore structure, size and surface area (Yang et al., 2021), along with the electronic structure of active sites and the percentage of different metal valence states (Sha et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2021). SEM and TEM images demonstrated that N-CDs/FMOF-Zr maintained the sheet morphology of FMOF-Zr (refer to Fig. 1) and both of them displayed similar porous structure (Fig. S7), but its size decreased compared with pristine FMOF-Zr. The smaller sized materials have a larger surface-to-volume ratio, therefore increasing the specific surface area for boosting the metal active site exposure to promote mass transport and the reaction with substrates. Hence, N-CDs/FMOF-Zr with a larger surface area than FMOF-Zr (refer to Fig. S7) displayed enhanced catalytic activity. On the other hand, XPS spectra indicated that Fe2P peaks of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr shifted to lower energies (refer to Fig. 2 E) compared with those of FMOF-Zr, indicating that the introduction of N-CDs increased the electron density of Fe active sites. This was because the as-prepared N-CDs with amine functional groups can be served as an electron donator (Gedda et al., 2016) to increase the electron density of Fe active sites, contributing to breaking O-O bonds in H2O2 for efficient catalytic performance (Sha et al., 2022). Additionally, it is envisioned that the increased proportion of Fe2+ (Table S4) in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr from 51.89% to 72.42% could improve catalytic activity due to a fact that active species is mainly generated from a Fe2+-mediated Fenton reaction (Sha et al., 2022). This was verified by catalytic degradation of an organic dye of rhodamine B (RhB) because iron-containing nanomaterials showed a potential as a Fenton catalyst to degrade organic pollutants. Obviously, it can be observed from Fig. S11 A and B that N-CDs/FMOF-Zr exhibited a higher degradation rate than FMOF-Zr owing to lower absorbance at the same time, indicating that more free radicals were produced in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr catalytic system and accelerating the reaction. Moreover, the potentially increased •OH was monitored by terephthalic acid (TA), which was utilized as a dosimeter of •OH because TA can be oxidized by •OH to produce highly fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA) (Amirzehni et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. S11C, the enhancing effect of the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr on the fluorescence emission of the TA-H2O2 system was apparently stronger than that of pristine FMOF-Zr, further confirming the enhanced catalytic performance by the introduction of N-CDs. In summary, the enhanced catalytic activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr can be attributed to the synergistic effect of: (i) superimposed catalytic activity of N-CDs, (ii) higher surface area, (iii) increased electron density and (iiii) higher percentage of Fe2+. These factors collectively contributed to enhance catalytic and speed up reaction.

Considering that environmental factors (e. g. pH, nanozyme and substrate concentrations) would influence the catalytic activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr and ultimately impact the sensing performance, the detection conditions were systematically optimized. It can be seen from Fig. S12A that the maximum ΔA was obtained at pH = 4.0, which can be explained by the following reasons. GLP is a polyhydric acid with four acid dissociation constants (pKa) of 0.78, 2.29, 5.96 and 10.98 (Chen et al., 2019). Consequently, it displays as a cation specie when pH < pKa1= 0.78 and a neutral molecule when 0.78 < pH< pKa2= 2.29. Regarding pH > 2.29, the portion of GLP with an anionic form and corresponding charge intensities increase with the increase of pHs. In a word, in our tested experimental condition (pH=3.0–6.0), GLP was presented as an anionic state. Meanwhile, the point of zero charge (pHpzc) of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was determined to be 4.68. Accordingly, N-CDs/FMOF-Zr possessed a positive potential when pH< 4.68 and a negative charge under pH> 4.68, indicating electrostatic attraction would be occurred between GLP and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr under pH less than 4.68 (Fig. S13). Further, the positive charge of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr gradually increased with the increase of pH and reached the maximum at pH= 4.0. As a result, the capture of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr toward GLP gradually rose when pH ranged from 3.0 to 4.0 owing to electrostatic attraction and achieved the maximum at pH= 4.0, giving to the highest change of A652. In contrast, when pH> 4.0, the electrostatic attraction became weak and even electrostatic repulsion when pH>pHpzc of 4.68, leading to the decrease of ΔA. Further, Fig. S12B demonstrated that ΔA increased gradually when NaAc-HAc buffer concentration < 10 mmol/L, on the contrary, it began decreased when buffer concentration > 10 mmol/L and reached the highest change at 10 mmol/L NaAc-HAc buffer (P < 0.05). This because different buffer system would influence catalytic activity of nanozyme (Raineri et al., 2019) and then have effects on sensing. Therefore, 10 mmol/L NaAc-HAc buffer was chosen for GLP sensing. Fig. S12 C, D and E implied that the optimum nanozyme and substrate concentrations were 0.65 mg/mL of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, 2 mmol/L TMB and 50 mmol/L H2O2 (P < 0.05). When the nanozyme or substrate concentrations were low, the excess GLP may not be completely recognized, reducing the accuracy of detection. When these concentrations were too high, low concentration of GLP may not trigger significant activity inhibition, which is not conducive to low concentration detection and reduces the detection sensitivity. Therefore, N-CDs/FMOF-Zr concentration of 0.65 mg/mL, 2 mmol/L TMB and 50 mmol/L H2O2 was selected to construct the GLP sensing platform. As seen from Fig. S12F, the ΔA dramatically increased in the initial 1 min and reached a steady state after incubation of 5 min (P > 0.05), indicating that 5 min was enough for N-CDs/FMOF-Zr to interact with GLP.

N-CDs/FMOF-Zr with POD-like activity can oxidize TMB into oxTMB, which possessed characteristic adsorption at 652 nm and thus displayed a blue color. GLP can inhibit its POD-like activity and thus give rise to a decrease of A652 and a faded color evolution. Under optimal conditions, GLP standard solutions with varied concentrations were mixed with N-CDs/FMOF-Zr in 10 mmol/L NaAc-HAc buffer (pH=4.0), followed by incubation for 5 min at room temperature to inhibit the POD-like activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. And then, TMB and H2O2 were subsequently added into these systems to react at 35 °C for 10 min. Finally, the solution color and UV–vis absorbance spectra were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 5 A, the absorbance at 652 nm gradually decreased with the increase of GLP concentrations, followed by an obvious color change from blue to colorless (the inset). Further, the related fit plot in Fig. 5 B demonstrated that A652 displayed an admirable linear response toward GLP concentrations ranging from 0.039 to 3.19 µg/mL. The corresponding regression equation was estimated as Y= −0.2305[C]+ 0.9059 ([C]: GLP concentration, µg/mL) with R2 = 0.9962. The limit of detection (LOD) of the colorimetric sensing was calculated to be 0.0131 µg/mL according to 3σ/k (σ: standard deviation of 9 blanks; k: the slope of the calibration curve). Notably, the repeatability of the colorimetric mode sensing was studied by replicate detection of the A652 of the sensing system at the same concentration of 1 µg/mL GLP (n = 9). According to the results (Fig. 5 C), the relative standard deviation (RSD) of these nine sample solutions was 3.28% (< 5%), clarifying that the colorimetric sensor we developed has good reproducibility.

Besides as colorimetric chromatic substrate, TMB also possesses bright blue fluorescence (FL) emission (Dong et al., 2019), implying its potential to be served as FL signal for constructing FL sensing. The feasibility and principle of the fluorescence mode were investigated. Herein, TMB solution exhibited a FL emission at 406 nm under excitation of 300 nm (Fig. S14A). It should be noted that nanomaterials can be employed as nanoquenchers to quench FL of a specific fluorophore mainly via inner filter effect (IFE) and Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) when spectra overlap occurs between absorbance spectra of the nanomaterial and excitation/emission spectra of the fluorophore (Liu et al., 2016). UV–vis and FL spectra of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr were studied to investigate whether N-CDs/FMOF-Zr had an effect on the FL of TMB. As shown in Fig. S14A, the resultant N-CDs/FMOF-Zr possessed no characteristic FL emission (black line). Further, its UV–vis absorbance demonstrated that N-CDs/FMOF-Zr had an obvious characteristic peak at ∼200 nm (red line). Accordingly, the excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) of TMB solution were collected. Obviously, there was no obvious spectral overlapping between UV–vis absorption of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr and FL excitation/emission spectra of TMB, ruling out the presence of IFE and FRET. Additionally, as expected, the FL intensity of TMB showed no significant decrease upon the addition of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr into the TMB solution (Fig. S14B). Consequently, it can be inferred that N-CDs/FMOF-Zr has no significant interference with the fluorescence of TMB. Interestingly, when in the presence of catalysts, TMB was oxidized into oxTMB and accordingly resulted in FL decrease. Consequently, when TMB was incubated with N-CDs/FMOF-Zr in the presence of H2O2, the FL intensity of the sensing system dramatically decreased. GLP can inhibit the POD-like of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, preventing TMB from being oxidized into oxTMB and resulting in FL enhancement. As a result, the FL of the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr+TMB+H2O2 sensing system gradually increased with increasing GLP concentrations. Based on these facts, a fluorescent “turn-on” sensing for GLP can be developed by utilizing TMB as a fluorescence indicator. Fluorescent tests were conducted at an excitation wavelength of 300 nm and 10 nm of excitation and emission slit width. The other conditions were identical to those used in the above-mentioned colorimetric sensing experiments. The fluorescent spectra of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr+TMB+H2O2 sensing system in the presence of different concentrations of GLP were shown in Fig. 5 D. It was seen from Fig. 5 D that the intensity of FL spectra at 406 nm (F406) gradually increased when GLP concentration increased from 0 to 6.38 µg/mL. Additionally, a favorable linear plot (Fig. 5 E) in the range of 0.0088–3.98 µg/mL was obtained by fitting GLP concentrations versus F406, which can be described as an equation of Y= 109.89 [C]+ 200.10 (R2 =0.9915). Accordingly, the LOD of FL sensing mode was estimated as 0.0015 µg/mL. The lower LOD and wider linear range of fluorescent mode than colorimetric mode in the case also verified high sensitivity of fluorescent analysis, which was consistent with previous works (Liu et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2021). Similarly, the reproducibility of the FL mode was also verified by the afore-mentioned method. A good repeatability of FL mode (Fig. 5 F) was demonstrated through a low RSD of 2.84% (n = 9).

Previous studies have revealed that the oxTMB exhibited not only color changes, but also a superior near-infrared (NIR) laser-driven photothermal effect that can convert the excitation energy of NIR laser into heat (temperature) (Fu et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2020). Thus, oxTMB was an alternative photothermal agent for various photothermal applications, such as photothermal temperature sensing. Herein, we utilized N-CDs/FMOF-Zr as a peroxidase (POD) mimic to catalyze the oxidation of TMB into oxTMB in the presence of H2O2. Accordingly, a temperature increase can be observed owing to the photothermal effect of oxTMB. GLP can inhibit its catalytic activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr to prevent the transformation of TMB into oxTMB and further cause a temperature decrease. Therefore, the temperature of the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr+TMB+H2O2 sensing system gradually decreased with the increasing concentrations of GLP. Accordingly, a photothermal temperature assay platform for GLP can be developed by virtue of the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr-mediated TMB-H2O2 system. However, due to the lack of integrated photothermal sensing modules, most current photothermal platforms can only test samples one at a time rather than batch testing (Guo et al., 2020). Herein, for further miniaturization of photothermal detection, a home-made photothermal device was also constructed for high-throughput assay (Guo et al., 2020). As depicted in Fig. 6 A and Fig. S15, the device consisted of a 3D printed sample bracket, three separable sample slots for 96-well plates, 808 nm lasers with a power density of 2 W/cm2 and thermometers. First, the feasibility of the NIR laser-driven photothermal effect of oxTMB mediated by N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was investigated. The N-CDs/FMOF-Zr+TMB+H2O2 +NaAc-HAc system presented the highest temperature change (ΔT) about 14.2 ℃ before and after laser irradiation, as shown in Fig. 6 B, while NaAc-HAc buffer had no significant change. When GLP was added, the temperature of the sensing system decreased and resulted in a relatively low ΔT due to the decrease of oxTMB. As a result, oxTMB can be utilized as a highly sensitive photothermal indicator to construct a sensitive photothermal sensing platform to detect GLP, in which the temperature signal can be easily read by a thermometer. The irradiation time was investigated (Fig. 6 C) before constructing a photothermal sensing method. The temperature of sensing system gradually increased with the prolonged irradiation time, especially at the initial 6 min, and it was stable after 9 min of irradiation. Thus, subsequent photothermal experiments were conducted at an irradiation time of 9 min.

Next, we systematically studied the analytical performance for GLP photothermal detection by recording the temperature changes of the system at different concentrations of GLP. As shown in Fig. 6 D, the temperature of the sensing system gradually decreased with the GLP concentrations increased from 0 to 6.38 µg/mL. Besides, in the concentration range of 0.016–3.98 µg/mL (Fig. 6 E), GLP concentrations and the change of temperature (ΔT) had a good linear relationship (R2 =0.9922). The related linear regression equation was Y= −1.3856 [C]+ 12.336, and the LOD of this mode was 0.0115 µg/mL. Finally, the good reproducibility was also evidenced by a low RSD of 4.24% for nine samples (Fig. 6 F).

In a word, the proposed colorimetric, FL and photothermal temperature tri-mode sensing platform exhibited favorable analytical performance for detecting GLP by comparing it with the previous reports (Table S5). Furthermore, multimodal sensing was capable of providing multidimensional results and exhibited more adaptability for practical analytical applications, which can satisfy different testing requirements according to sensitivity and available instruments or meters.

To unveil the fundamental mechanism, the interplay between GLP and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was systematically inspected. It is believed that the inhibition of nanozyme activity by targets can occur via two main mechanisms: (i) structure decomposition of nanozyme triggered by analytes (Naveen Prasad et al., 2021) and (ii) the adsorption/interaction between analytes and nanozyme results in suppression of the nanozyme's catalytic activity (Chang et al., 2016). Firstly, the N-CDs-FMOF-Zr still maintained its nanosheet morphology after interacting with GLP (Fig. S16). Besides, XRD patterns of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr before and after incubation with GLP were identical ( Fig. 7 A). Overall, it can be ruled out enzyme activity inhibition resulted from nanozyme framework collapse.

Accordingly, adsorption/interaction between GLP and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was speculated as a reasonable inhibition mechanism, which can be verified by Zeta potential, FTIR, XPS and BET results. Firstly, both BET surface area and pore volume of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr decreased after incubating with GLP (Table S6), which may be due to sites occupation by GLP, providing initiatory evidence for absorption/capture of GLP (Zhu et al., 2015). According to pKa of GLP (0.78, 2.29, 5.96 and 10.98) (Chen et al., 2019), it was presented as an anionic state in our tested condition (pH=4.0). While N-CDs/FMOF-Zr possessed a positive Zeta potential of 27.23 mV, which decreased to − 3.67 mV after interacting with GLP (Fig. S17), suggesting an electrostatic interaction between them. As FTIR spectra presented in Fig. 7 B, the decrease of Zr-O (775 and 680 cm−1) and Fe-O (565 cm−1) bonds proved the participation of M-O (M=Zr/Fe) bonds for GLP capture. Besides, characteristic peaks in 1635–1571 cm−1 and 1394–1332 cm−1 generated from CO vibrations of ferrocenedicarboxylic groups changed after incubating with GLP, indicating that the CO groups such as carboxyl were also involved in GLP recognition. XPS characterizations of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr before and after incubating with GLP were further conducted to disclose the host-guest interactions between them. Fig. 7 C demonstrated that a new peak of characteristic P2p appeared at 132.58 eV in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr + GLP. Besides, the high-resolution of P2p (Fig. S18) demonstrated that P2p peak can be fitted with two peaks at 132.18 and 133.02 eV, which corresponded to the PO bond in GLP molecule and the M-O-P (M=Zr and Fe) formed between the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr sensor and GLP, respectively. These results clarified that GLP was successfully attached to N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. The deconvoluted N1s spectra (Fig. 7 D) of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was split into three peaks of C-N (398.55 eV), CN (399.41 eV) and N-H (401.17 eV). These peaks presented a red-shift after reacting with GLP, implying the decrease of N electron density, which may be caused by hydrogen bonding interaction between GLP and N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. Similarly, the peaks of CO and C-O in O1s spectra (Fig. 7 E) shifted to higher energies associated with a decreased proportion (Table S7). Consequently, these findings suggested that N-CDs/FMOF-Zr can capture GLP via hydrogen bonding interaction. Besides, the Zr-O and Fe-O peaks in O1s also exhibited red shifts and decreased percentages after incubating with GLP. Meanwhile, a new peak associated with M-OP (M=Zr and Fe) species can be observed at 530.84 eV in the O1s spectra of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr in the presence of GLP. These changes could be caused by the surface complexation between GLP and the metal active sites of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, and then formed metal-O-P/PO. Further, the Zr3d spectra (Fig. 7 F) shifted to higher binding energies and its corresponding proportion also declined (Table S7). More significantly, a new peak corresponding to Zr-O-P/PO was formed, indicating that Zr-O clusters in N-CDs/FMOF-Zr can act as tentacles to bind with GLP. On the other hand, as mentioned above, the POD-like activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was generated from the electron recycling between a redox pair of Fe2+/Fe3+, in which the rate-limiting step is the reduction of Fe3+ by H2O2 (Li J. et al., 2021). GLP can strongly chelate with Fe3+ species (Sun et al., 2021) to form a Fe3+-GLP complex, which can be served as a redox-insert complex and alter the redox potential of Fe3+ towards H2O2, impairing the efficiency of Fe2+ regeneration (Tai et al., 2022). As a result, GLP would block the cycling of the redox doublet of Fe2+/Fe3+, leading to inhibition of POD-like activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr.

Accordingly, multiple physical interactions (electrostatic attraction and hydrogen-bonding) as well as M-O-P/PO (M=Zr, Fe) chemical interactions promoted rapid the adsorption of GLP onto N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. The selective adsorption process led to GLP occupying the active sites of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr, thus hindering the catalytic decomposition of H2O2 into ROS (Luo et al., 2021). In addition, the complexation between GLP and Fe3+ species can decrease the efficiency of Fe2+ regeneration and would block the cycling of the redox doublet of Fe2+/Fe3+ to suppress catalytic activity. Overall, the inhibition mechanism was attributed to the synergetic effects of adsorption and interactions between N-CDs/FMOF-Zr and GLP.

The selectivity and anti-interference ability of the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr nanozyme-mediated sensing were verified by evaluating its response toward other pesticides and co-existing components in food samples in the absence and presence of GLP. It can be obviously observed from Fig. S19 (purple bar) that only GLP can inhibit the POD-like activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr and further cause the significant decrease of absorbance at 652 nm. However, no obvious catalytic inhabitation was observed upon the addition of other interferences, implying that the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr sensor displayed high selectivity to GLP. Moreover, compared with the sole presence of GLP, there was no significant difference in POD-like activity when these interferences co-existed with GLP (blue bar), demonstrating good anti-interference ability. Besides, the insets (top image) indicated that the color of the sensing systems obviously changed from blue to colorless by GLP while the color remained blue after introducing other interferences. When adding interferences into GLP containing sensing systems, all of the solutions undergo color fading (bottom image). Four kinds of non-organophosphorus pesticides including 2, 4-dichlorphenoxyacetic acid (2, 4-D), thiophanate-methyl (TM), carbendazim (MBC) and carbaryl showed no obvious inhibition effect on N-CDs/FMOF-Zr catalytic activity due to the absence of phosphate groups in structures of these pesticides and thus they cannot be selectively interacted with metal active sites. In term of other organophosphorus pesticides, despite of the presence of phosphate groups on their molecule structures (Fig. S20), methyl of these organophosphorus pesticides generated strong steric hindrance (Wang L. et al., 2016). Besides methyl, their molecule structures were more complex than GLP, and thus steric hindrance effect was accordingly stronger (Singh et al., 2015). These factors resulted in a weaker interaction between these organophosphorus pesticides and metal (M=Zr and Fe) active sites. Therefore, as an organophosphorus pesticide with the simplest molecular structure, GLP has the strongest inhibitory effect on the activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr. These facts revealed that this sensor can selectively detect GLP and the other tested interferents had a negligible effect, indicating good selectivity and anti-interference ability of the sensing system and its potential application in practical samples.

Reusability of a nanozyme-mediated nanosensor is a critical factor regarding affordability for practical applications because good reusability could reduce the test cost by utilizing the same setup repeatedly (Das et al., 2021). The reusability test of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr was studied by successively washing the spent N-CDs/FMOF-Zr using ethanol and 10 mmol/L NaOH as eluents to remove the absorbed oxTMB and GLP, followed by centrifuging and further washing with H2O. Obviously, it can be seen from Fig. S21 that the POD-like catalytic activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr still maintained 86.58% of the first recycling after four successive regenerations, indicating its good reusability of the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr.

Firstly, the matrix effect (ME) of real samples on the assay has been evaluated by constructing calibration curves in different matrices and then comparing the slope of matrix standard curve (Smatrix) with the slope of aqueous solvent standard curve (SH2O). The specific calculation formula was expressed as ME= (Smatrix/SH2O-1) × 100%. When 0 ≤ |ME= ≤20%, it was regarded as a weak matrix effect and 20%< |ME= <50% indicated a relatively strong matrix effect. When |ME= ≥50%, it was considered as a strong matrix effect. According to the slope calculation shown in Table S8, ME of GLP showed a weak matrix effect in different matrices.

To verify the practicability of this multi-mode sensing platform for GLP detection, we performed measurements on real samples (rice, millet and soybean) (detailed pretreatment refer to SI) by a standard addition method. Firstly, HPLC-MS/MS was conducted to confirm the blank content of GLP in these samples, and the results (Fig. S22) illustrated that these samples were not contaminated with GLP. Therefore, spiked recovery experiments were carried out by spiking GLP standard solution into pretreated real samples to investigate its practical application potential of our proposed method and the results were tabulated in Table S9. Obviously, favorable recoveries of 92.83–108.80% (RSD < 4.74%), 90.64–107.04% (RSD < 6.52%) and 90.78–107.24% (RSD < 4.89%) were respectively obtained by colorimetric, FL and temperature readouts, indicating desirable precision and accuracy. Besides, HPLC-MS/MS (regression equation: y = 0.06883x-0.02756, R2 =0.99912) was employed as a standard method to estimate its practical reliability of the tri-node sensing platform. As revealed in Table S10, GLP concentrations detected by three readouts were coincident with HPLC-MS/MS (P > 0.05). Overall, the TMB-mediated multi-mode platform showed great promise for GLP quantification in food metrices with high precision, accuracy, and reliability.

In this study, a versatile tri-mode sensing platform was developed by the N-CDs/FMOF-Zr nanosensor with POD mimicking to sensitive, visual and portable detection GLP via multidimensional readouts. The sensing strategy was realized by a TMB-mediated nanozyme activity modulation, in which GLP can inhibit POD-like activity of N-CDs/FMOF-Zr by adsorption and interactions. Owing to excellent FL property of TMB as well as colorimetric and NIR-region photothermal effect of oxTMB, the platform can provide colorimetric, FL and photothermal temperature three signals. These signals can be readout by a microplate reader, FL spectrophotometer and portable thermometer, realizing different sensitivity and availability to satisfy different test demands. To summarize, this research illuminated a promising manner to develop a multidimensional sensing platform, in which different reading modes can be selected according to different situations for reliable, sensitive, portable detection of GLP.

Glyphosate (GLP) is one of the most widely used herbicides in agriculture, however, its residue in environmental and food matrices has pose a threat to public health. For GLP monitoring, there is still considerable gap between laboratorial analysis and real demands. Herein, a colorimetric, fluorescence and photothermal temperature multi-signals integrated platform was developed for visual, sensitive and portable GLP detection. Additionally, a home-made photothermal mini-device was constructed to realize robust and on-site GLP sensing. Overall, the multimodal sensing platform was more adaptability for practical applications particularly for resource-limited situations, which can satisfy different testing according to sensitivity and available instruments/meters.

Xueli Luo: Conceptualization, Design and Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft & editing. Gengli Huang: Formal analysis. Chenxu Bai and Chenyu Tang : Data curation. Youwen Tan: Software. Chunyan Wang, Ying Yu and Jia Kong : Review & editing. Jihong Huang and Zhonghong Li : Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.
